title: ExoMol line lists -- LX. Molecular line list for the ammonia isotopologue 15NH3

journal: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
abstract: A theoretical line list for 15NH3 CoYuTe-15 is presented based on the empirical potential energy and ab initio dipole moments surfaces developed and used for the production of the ExoMol line list CoYuTe for 14NH3. The ro-vibrational energy levels and wavefunctions are computed using the variational program TROVE. The line list ranges up to 10 000 cm-1 ( m) and contains 929 795 249 transitions between 1 269 961 states with . The line list should be applicable for temperatures up to 1000 K. To improve the accuracy of the line positions, a set of experimentally-derived energy levels of 15NH3 is produced using the \ procedure. To this end, 37 experimental sources of the line positions of 15NH3 available in the literature are collected, combined and systematised to produce a self-consistent spectroscopic network of 21 095 15NH3 transitions covering 40 vibrational bands ranging up to 6818 cm-1 and resulting in 2777 energy term values. These \ energies are then used to replace the theoretical values in the CoYuTe-15 line list and also complemented by pseudo- \ energies obtained by an isotopologue extrapolation using the previously reported \ energies of the 14NH3 parent isotopologue of ammonia. A list of 53 856 high resolution transitions between \ states and theoretical intensities is provided in the HITRAN format. Comparison with the recent experimental spectra of 15NH3 illustrate the potential of the line list for detections and as an efficient assistant in spectroscopic assignments. The line list is available from www.exomol.com.
body_text:
Introduction

3 15 has long been detected in interstellar molecular clouds where it is used as a tracer of the 15N/14N isotopic ratio
, planetary atmospheres
 and the Earth atmosphere , in meteorites , and in comets . Very recently, detected 3 15 in the atmosphere of a cool brown dwarf with with the Mid-Infrared Instrument of JWST.

This work is an update of our previous empirical room temperature line lists for 3 15 as well line lists by .
 Here we present an extended variationally computed line list for 3 15 covering the rotational excitations up to and the wavenumber range up to 10000 \ ( m). The line list is computed using the variational program \ and the same computational set up used by to produce a hot line list for 3 14 called CoYuTe. The line list is then improved using a MARVELisation procedure, where the theoretical energies of 3 15 are replaced by experimentally-derived values obtained using the \ procedure . To this end, an extensive set of experimental spectroscopic line positions of 3 15 has been collected from the literature covering publication from 1947 (see Section ), combined and organised to produce a self-consistent spectroscopic network and then used to produce experimentally derived \ energies of 3 15 .
A new simplified and self-consistent reduced scheme to represent the ro-vibrational quantum numbers of 3 15 is suggested consisting of six quantum labels.

In order to improve more calculated energies that do not have MARVEL counterparts, the so-called isotopologue interpolation (IE) procedure is employed. This procedure takes advantage of extensive experimental spectroscopic data available for the main isotopologue of a molecule and an observation that errors of the calculated ro-vibrational energies computed the same \ models are transferable between isotopologues of the same type .

The line list, comprising the ro-vibrational energies, lifetimes, Einstein coefficients, partition functions as well as temperature and pressure dependent opacity tables is provided from the ExoMol data base at www.exomol.com. The line list is validated by comparing to HITRAN as well as to experimental spectra of 3 15 .

For the construction of the line list for 3 15 we followed the following steps as outlined in the work-flow diagram in Fig. : (i) compilation of experimental line positions of 3 15 from the literature and construction of experimentally derived energies using the MARVEL procedure ; (ii) Variational calculations of ro-vibrational energies and Einstein A coefficients using the program \ ; (iii) application of the isotopologue extrapolation technique and (iv) production of the line list.

 \ procedure

 

The approach and input structure

The algorithm used for deriving empirical energy levels of 3 15 in this work is the Measured Active Rotation Vibration Energy Level ( ) algorithm, developed by Furtenbacher and co-workers . The initial step in the \ methodology involves compiling a dataset of measured high-resolution spectroscopic transition frequencies commonly referred to as line positions.

The line positions used as an input for \ need to feature quantum numbers (QNs) assigned to both upper and lower states, and to ensure uniformity, a consistent set of quantum numbers is applied to all transitions. Furthermore, each line used is accompanied by its respective experimental uncertainty. The transitions are distinctly tagged, indicating the experimental source, and assigned a specific identifier within that source. Lastly, through the implementation of a weighted linear least-squares inversion procedure, \ extracts a set of energy levels along with their corresponding uncertainties;
here these uncertainties are actually recomputed using a bootstrap procedure .

The goal is to create a cohesive network of energy levels, referred to as a spectroscopic network . The transition uncertainties are systematically improved and incorrect lines are simultaneously removed from the database. In more detail, a continuous assessment of the self-consistency of experimental transition frequencies is conducted. \ identifies outliers, often caused by inaccurately assigned wavenumbers or during the transcription of the measured data into the transition list. In particular, transitions are flagged as invalid if the ratio between the optimal uncertainty and the original uncertainty exceeds a threshold of 100. These invalidated transitions are either corrected or annotated with a negative sign applied to their wavenumber values, to exclude them from further analysis by .

Previously, the transition uncertainties were often manually increased in order to find the optimal set of uncertainties. However, the latest version of \ offers a bootstrap method to iteratively determine the optimal energy level uncertainties . With the bootstrap feature of 4 the individual uncertainties of the \ energies are determined to be suitable and reasonable. This is the method that was used in this work.

The 4 Online version which was used in the current study offers flexibility in handling transition energy units in comparison to earlier versions of \ that were limited in only using . 4 Online supports various units ( , Hz, kHz, MHz, GHz, and THz). To enable this expanded functionality, a segment file is included as an input, containing a compilation of the experimental source names with their respective units.

NH3 quantum numbers

For the rotation and vibration states and motion of the pyramid like ammonia molecule with a feasible inversion mode, the 3(M) molecular symmetry group is used. This group consists of six irreducible representations, , , , , and , referred to below also as symmetries. As for 3 14 , the ro-vibrational states of 3 15 with and symmetries do not exist due to the Pauli principle .

Vibrational quantum numbers

There are four vibrational modes in ammonia, (symmetric stretch), (inversion), (asymmetric stretch) and (asymmetric bend), where and are doubly degenerate.
The corresponding normal mode vibrational quantum numbers are , , , , , , and s/a where ( ) are the vibrational quanta, and are the corresponding vibrational angular momentum quantum numbers with as the total vibrational angular momentum quantum number and the labels s and a are used to indicate if the inversion mode is the symmetric or asymmetric, respectively. The values of ( ) satisfy
 

All excitations of transform as in 3(M). The symmetric property of the symmetric bending mode upon the inversion (symmetric or asymmetric) provides the s/a labels to the full vibrational state. Inversion state of type ( ) transform as (s) or (a) in 3(M).

The values of have a direct correlation with the symmetry of the corresponding contribution, based on the simple rule of multiple of three: states with have the symmetry forming degenerate pairs of the same values of . States with have symmetries or depending on the state parity or , respectively, see Table .

The total vibrational symmetry is then the product of irreducible representations from all four modes , after the associated reduction to the irreducible representation. For example, the state ( ) has the symmetry as a direct symmetry product , the state has the symmetry as the product , while a more complex example spans three states: , and as the result of the product 
 
The latter can be also interpreted as a the result of the combination of the bending and stretching vibrational angular momenta and , respectively:
 
Here, as a total vibrational angular momentum is important to resolve the degeneracy of the three distinct components of the combinations in the , which is necessary for the for the non-degenerate states. The same result, in principle, is achieved by using the total vibrational symmetry , with and ( or ) to account for the two non degenerate components of this combination. In fact, using symmetries is more physically sensible than the non-physical alternative with the defined signs , but the former is more popular in experimental studies of ammonia and spherical tops in general.

 

Rotational quantum numbers

The rotational assignment follows the rigid rotor wavefunctions , where is the total angular momentum quantum number, is the projection of the total angular momentum on the laboratory fixed axis and is the projection of the total angular momentum on the molecular fixed axis . Since does not play any role in the classification of the rotational states in the absence of external fields, it is commonly omitted from the consideration.

In order to correlate to the molecular symmetry, the rigid rotor wavefunctions are first combined into symmetric and asymmetric Wang-type functions 
 
where we introduced and to describe the parity of the rotational state and dropped . The symmetries associated with different values of and are explained in Table . Although just two quantities, and are sufficient for the description of the degenerate states, when , one more descriptor is required. In experimental studies, it is usually the sign of that serves this purpose, which is however not well defied and often lead to ambiguity in descriptions of the experimental data from different sources. Here we use the symmetries = and to distinguish different states of the same . This symmetry label is also used for states with when for consistency.

Ro-vibrational quantum numbers

It should be noted that the absolute signs of , or are also ill-defined. Indeed, the positive and negative values of (and also , see below) contribute equally to the description of ro-vibrational states of symmetric tops giving rise to the so-called Wang 50/50 mixtures, see Eq. . The relative signs however do matter, which is the main reason for the signed values of s commonly used in the spectroscopic analysis.

Another reason is that the sign of or is effectively an additional quantum label allowing to distinguish states of the same absolute or , even though they often lead to conflicting assignments of the same spectra from different experimental sources.
Ammonia is characterised by its tunneling motion which leads to inversion of the molecule and splitting of the levels into symmetric/antisymmetric combinations which we denote s/a. Depending on the choice of QNs, s/a can be redundant but still useful as the inversion splitting is a key motif of ammonia spectra.

Our quantum numbers of choice are the following (unambiguous) quantities: , , , , , , , (i.e. absolute values of the corresponding vibrational angular momenta), (absolute value of the rotational quantum number), irreducible representations , and of the total ro-vibrational state, as well as of the vibrational and rotational contributions and s/a. These quantum numbers correspond closely to the ones recommended by and used in the previous
 \ studies of 3 14 .

It is now becoming more and more common to use simplified, compact schemes for assigning ro-vibrational states where several quantum numbers are combined into a single counting index. For example, in recent experimental studies of CH4 (see ), it is a counting ro-vibrational index within the same , total symmetry and the polyad . In the case of N2O , this is the index also within the same , (vibrational angular momentum) and polyad . As will be discussed below, the vibrational quantum numbers of ammonia become ambiguous at high vibrational excitations as they are coupled through rotational interactions, which mix vibrational states from the same and different polyads. Moreover, the coupling scheme between different degrees of freedom are complicated by the symmetry product rules making the usage of the vibrational quantum number less intuitive. We therefore decided to introduce a reduced QN scheme, with the minimal number of quantum indices that maintain the key aspects of the rotation-vibrational states. One of such aspects we would like to preserve is the description of the individual vibrational ( ) and rotational state contributions we would like to preserve. While the rotational part in the case of the symmetric top ammonia can be mainly described by the rotational quantum numbers and , as a compact representation of the full set of the vibrational quantum numbers , we introduce a single vibrational index . This index counts vibrational s/a pairs if states sorted according to their increased energies:
 
Note that it does not contain s/a, i.e. the full inversion-vibrational description is according with .
Thus, our final set of the ro-vibrational quantum numbers consists of the following six labels given by:
 
where we included the rotational symmetry in order to resolve the ambiguity occurring at for the non-degenerate components. While we replace the full vibrational description by in the \ set for the sake of its compactness, the original eight-QN information is retained by providing a detailed correlation table between and , which is given below in Section .

Selection rules

The standard rigorous (electric dipole, single photon) selection rules in the case of ammonia spectra are
 
where and do not exist due to the Pauli principle (the corresponding nuclear statistical weights are zero). Since the parity ( or ) changes through a electric dipole, single photon transition, which is also directly correlated with the s/a characteristics, one can also obtain the following rigorous rules for s/a:
 
This is because flips the parity of the vibrational states when is odd. For the two-photon transitions, the selection rules are
 

A number of two-photon spectra have been recorded experimentally , but no transitions with have been observed; these transitions are allowed but expected to be too weak to
readily observable .

Most of the experimental data collected in this work are from single-photon electric dipole spectra.
There are no transitions between states of para and ortho states and therefore a single estimated ortho-para transition frequency (magic number) was required to connect the ortho- and para- networks (see below).

Ammonia has a rather strong propensity rule which can cause networks to fragment and problems with effective Hamiltonian representations, see for a discussion of this issue. For the current dataset, half of transitions (49\%) are with , 37\% with , 8\% with and only 6\% is for all other transitions .

Experimental data sources

In the following, the existing experimental sources containing transition frequencies of 3 15 are reviewed. Detailed statistics, including the number of lines considered and validated, are presented in Table .

Here it was important to include the vibrational symmetry into the state assignment for states with due to the degeneracy associated with states of / . We used \ quantum numbers and energies for reconstructing the state symmetries for all states.

Stated uncertainty = 0.0001 , which we doubled for blended lines. 1883 transitions were validated out of 1926. Some 's were changed to match more recent experimental assignment (19CaLoFaTa) if the upper state energies in the associated combination differences (CDs) matched. The upper values of "s/a" were assigned using the rule of ( ) should odd/even for "s/a" to change/not to change.

The uncertainties were set to 0.05 , but the data appear to be of lower quality and inconsistent with this uncertainty; they conflict with many other observations leading to large differences in CDs, significantly higher this the uncertainty. Only 385 out of 775 transitions could be validated.

Accurate ground state (g.s.) energies and energies are reported, which were them used in the combination differences calculations. We changed some s to match more recent data of based on the CDs (i.e. on the match of the upper state energies). Some of the states were reassigned to using \ matches and to agree with CDs from other sources. Many of the uncertanties were increased to 0.1 \ based on the CDs analysis.
Only 8 transitions could not be validated out of 807.

Uncertanties are provided.

The assignments are very limited and we could not make them work so this dataset was excluded.

Large uncertainties of 0.05 \ were assumed due to 2 decimal places given. Some are pure rotational within g.s. and some are hot within . Not all lines could be validated.

94UrKlYa Far-infrared ground state rotation-inversion transitions. Uncertainty is stated to be 0.000003 .

96BrMa.22 excluded because it disagrees with 96BrMa.18. Reported uncertainty 0.0003 \ in the paper.

00AnJoLeSa and 00AnJoLeSa(IR) form . 00AnJoLeSa contains Raman (two-photon forbidden) transitions; 00AnJoLeSa.72 is from a nonphysical state ( , g.s.) and was excluded.
00AnJoLeSa(IR) contains single photon (IR) transitions. We separated these transitions into two parts to facilitate handling these two distinct data sets.

06LeLiLiXu The band. Some transitions were reassigned to and to match .

16FoVaRiHe . Transitions from the band are reported but without specific assignment of the individual bands, i.e. all transitions are initially assigned to . Some of the lines were vibrationally reassigned by a combined CD analysis with the assigned data from 08LeLiXu covering the same upper states. Many lines even with multiple CDs could not be validated and were excluded as they lead to upper energies which conflict with those given by other sources and our \ calculations.

19CaLoFuTa(MHz) and 19CaLoFuTa, from . 19CaLoFuTa(MHz) contains data in MHz, while 19CaLoFuTa reports transitions from the , and bands are in . We separate 19CaLoFuTa (MHz) from 19CaLoFuTa ( ) because of the structure of the 4 input requiring the data in the original units. Only 10 transitions out of 7563 could not be validated.

20CaLoFu . This a second largest source reporting 5770 transitions from the bands and . About 250 transitions could not be validated because of conflicts with the experimental data from other sources as well as unusually large differences with our theoretical ( ) estimates. Among those cases, the most unexpected disagreement was found for five energy levels of 20CaLoFu ( ), all supported by multiple (up to 9) transitions with CDs within about 0.02 , that conflicted with the corresponding theoretical energies of \ by more than 8-16 . Considering that in all other cases, \ energies agree with \ to much better than 1 , these 5 groups of transitions were first considered as suspicious and then treated as experimental outliers, possibly caused by impurities present in experimental spectra of . Moreover, in all these case, we could find at least transition from 82SaHaAmSh from the same levels that agreed with \ within 0.01 . An example of such suspected false CDs is illustrated in Table , where we show 8 transitions from 20CaLoFu with a common upper state , and 5 ( ). The corresponding combination seemingly agree with each other leading to an upper energy term value of 2874.127 . This prediction is however very far from the \ value of 2882.4683 , which we does not seem to be unrealistic considering the generally very good agreement for most of the \ energies (94\% are within 0.1 ), see Fig. , including the band. Moreover, the experimental data set 82DiFuTrMi also contains a transition to this state with the upper state energy of 2882.4596 , which is an excellent agreement with the \ prediction, see Table . We have encountered at least 4 similar cases with a large number of CDs ( 8), all for with from 20CaLoFu , with large differences from \ (8-16 ) and in every case with an alternative, single transition from 82DiFuTrMi in an excellent agreement with . Although this is not obvious, but we decided to trust the \ and 82DiFuTrMi predictions in those cases and excluded the corresponding transitions from .

 

 

By the \ definition, \ energy of the lowest state is set to zero, which in case of 3 15 is , , a, (0,0,00,00), i.e. the upper, asymmetric inversion component of the ground state state, with the symmetric component , , s, (0,0,00,00) non-existent due to the nuclear spin statistics. Because of this effect, the inversion splitting of ammonia 3 15 cannot be observed experimentally and is not present in our \ set.

In order to connect the ortho- and para- networks, we used a MAGIC number as an energy difference between lowest (ground state) energy levels of para- and ortho- states, , , s ( ) and , , a ( ), respectively, taken from the term values list by : = 15.391430 .

Figure shows the \ energies of 3 15 .

 

 

 \ and correlation between experiment and theory

The experimental transitions in our list all originate from six lower states, g.s., , , , and , which correspond to 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 and 6, respectively. We started by building combination differences for experimental transitions originating from g.s. and , where we could take advantage of the existing energy term values from . By applying these term values to the transitions from
, the combination differences for the upper state , , and could be constructed and the corresponding energies compared. This procedure helped identify and correct possible misassignments of many upper states. In turn, the corresponding term values could be used to construct combination differences for all other hot bands originated from these states.

To facilitate this procedure, the CoYuTe-15\ line list was extensively used. Our ultimate goal is to use the experimentally derived \ energies in place of the theoretical values where available. To this end, the \ energies need to be correlated to the calculated \ energies. Being able to compare the two sets of data to each other helps both ways, to establish the quality of the variational calculations but also resolve potential problems in the \ set.

Owing to the high quality of the underlying empirical potential energy surface of NH3, the theoretical ro-vibrational term values and associated combination differences were of reasonably high quality, see Fig. . To help match experiment and theory, the signs of different values of and , and sometimes of and in the experimental sources had to be reassigned to make them consistent between different experimental data. In many case, the associated (non-rigorous) quantum number had to be modified, including the vibrational ones, , , and . However, the rigorous quantum numbers and symmetry were not altered. In order to correlate to , some \ quantum had to be modified to make them unambiguous and consistent with the experiment.

As part of the process, a set of term values was compiled to be used as lower states for the experimental set of 3 15 we collected based on a mixture of the experimentally derived term values of 85UrCrPa (g.s. and ) and \ term values. We then were able to correlate all the upper and lower states from the experimental transitions to the \ ro-vibrational states and thus use the \ QNs as an independent self-consistent set of assignments. This procedure helped correlate the experimental transitions from different sources featuring the same states, correct assignments, identify and exclude outliers.

Once the full correlation was constructed, it was then possible to replace the experimental quantum numbers with our \ set of six quantum numbers in Eq. . This set is based on the collective vibrational quantum number , which is defined as a counting index of the vibrational ( , s) energy term values, including the non-existing and states. The latter are also important as they play the role of the `band centres' for the ro-vibrational term values or transition frequencies.
The correlation between and the vibrational quantum numbers is illustrated in Table for all vibrational states from our \ data. The full correlation table is given as supplementary material. We used the theoretical, CoYuTe-15, term values to establish the correlation between the vibration-inversion states and . Being theoretical, our quantum label has the caveat of depending on the order of the CoYuTe-15\ energies and is thus affected by its quality, especially at higher excitations.

 

As an illustration of the \ data structure, in Table we provide an extract from the main \ data set. In order to help the users with our compact assignment scheme, especially with the new counting index , in the supplementary material we also provide (i) the original CoYuTe-15\ vibrational term values is based on, accompanied by the mapping with the experimental quantum numbers from Eq. (see Table ); (ii) the experimental transitions using the native quantum numbers (subject to some corrections for internal consistency).

 

Line list production

 \ calculations

The CoYuTe-15\ line list has been produced using the variational code \ with the spectroscopic model from developed and used for the CoYuTe line list for 3 14 , where we switched to the (atomic) masses Da and Da. This spectroscopic model consists of an empirical potential energy surface (PES) fitted by and an \ electric dipole moment surface (DMS) from . For the details of the computational set up see , which we only briefly describe here.

The kinetic energy operator (KEO) was expanded in terms of five linearised coordinates , around a non-rigid (inversion) frame on a equidistant grid of 1000 points covering the umbrella coordinate from -55 to 55 . The umbrella coordinate is defined as an angle between any of the N-Hi modes and their trisector (see ). The five coordinates are constructed from the following valence coordinates: the three bond lengths and the asymmetric combinations and of the bond angles ( ) defined as
 
by expanding them in terms of the Cartesian displacements around the reference configuration and truncating after the first order, see .

The stretching basis functions , and , as well as the inversion basis functions ( ) are generated numerically using the Numerov-Cooley approach , while for the bending basis functions and , 1D Harmonic oscillator wavefunctions were used. These basis functions are then optimised via a contraction-symmetrisation procedure to form a symmetry adapted (3(M)) vibrational ( ) basis functions as a sum-of-products of ( ). At this stage, the vibrational basis functions are assigned the normal mode quantum numbers , , , by correlating our local node wavefunctions to the Harmonic-oscillator-like solutions. For the rotational basis set, symmetrised rigid-rotor wavefunctions as simple Wang combinations, see Eqs.(, ). The rotational and vibrational assignments are then propagated to the final ro-vibrational variational wavefunctions using the largest eigen-coefficient approach . The size of the vibrational basis set is controlled by the polyad number parameter defined as follows:
 
As in , was used. Another parameter controlling the ro-vibrational basis set size is the energy threshold .
For the Zero-Point-Energy defined at the energy of the (non-physical) , s, state relative to the minimum of the CoYuTe PES NH3 we obtained 8001.42 .

Figure shows how the calculated energy values with \ compare to the experimentally derived energy term values of 3 15 ( ) as obs.-calc. residuals. The majority of the residuals are below 0.1 . The majority of the values with large residuals correspond to the \ energies based on 1-2 transitions only.

 

Pseudo- : isotopologue energy extrapolation

One of the limitations of the \ technique is that the completeness of the \ energies depends on the completeness of the original experimental sources. It is not uncommon for the experimental data set to be patchy, with some lines missing, with ambiguous assignment or blended transitions. In attempt to fill gaps in the \ energy sets when MARVELising the line lists, different techniques can be employed . Here we consider the isotopologue energy level extrapolation technique, similar to the one employed in , where we leverage the present \ energy levels list for the 3 14 parent isotopologue . The isotopologue extrapolation (IE) technique uses the assumption that the calculation errors of ro-vibrational energies of different isotopologues based on the same Born-Oppenheimer PES are approximately uniform.

To this end, variational \ calculations are performed using the \ surface from , for both the 3 14 and 3 15 isotopologues. The \ ro-vibrational energies from both isotopologues are then matched to their corresponding \ energies from this study and on the parent isotopologue from . Here we opt for the \ PESs in place of (more accurate) empirical PESs in order to mitigate possible contamination of the Born-Oppenheimer original \ surface with any mass dependent effects absorbed into CoYuTe PES through fitting to the experiment in . The \ residuals (observed minus calculated) of each energy level included in \ are first computed for the main isotopologue 3 14 and then these obs.-calc. differences are added onto the \ energies of 3 15 . In this way we form isotope extrapolation (IE) \ levels for 3 15 enabling a semi-empirical determination of energy levels present in the 3 14 \ list but not yet determined for 3 15 .

To gauge the accuracy of the IE levels for 3 15 , we compare those levels present in our \ study, with their IE counterparts. The residuals between the \ energies 3 15 and the CoYuTe-15\ computed energies alongside the residuals of IE energies are given in Fig. . As can be seen, the difference in accuracy between the two is found to vary, and shows a vibrational dependence. For this reason, we only substitute IE levels in place of CoYuTe-15\ levels for bands which are known to provide increased accuracy over CoYuTe-15. For excited vibrational states, the only IE levels found to improve over CoYuTe-15\ belong to the ground state, the inversion mode and its overtones, and , summarised in Table , and indicated in Fig. as `IE Chosen'. The corresponding uncertainties, also listed in Table , were estimated by comparing to the \ values if available, which are indicated in Fig. as `IE Excluded'.

 

 

 

Line list

The CoYuTe-15\ line list for 3 15 was computed for covering the wavenumber range up to 10000 , with the upper/lower energies limited by 18000/8000 , respectively. Intensity (Einstein A coefficients) calculations were performed on a GPU cluster using the GAIN MPI code .

The line list contains 929 795 249 transitions between 12 699 617 ro-vibrational states and consists of a States file, Transitions files and Partition function file. According with the ExoMolHD standards , we use experimentally derived energies to replace the theoretical values, where available, the procedure commonly referenced to as `MARVELisation'. From the \ set of 2777 energies, we selected 2754 values with residuals smaller than 1 \ from the \ energy term value to avoid any accidental \ outliers. These were complemented by 326 IE values and finally with a fourteen Effective Hamiltonian term values from .

Extracts from the States and Transition files are illustrated in Tables and . We follow the energy convention used for ammonia, where the lowest energy is the energy of the non-physical ground state , , s, (0,0,00,00), which is set to zero. In this convention, the lowest observable state , , a, (0,0,00,00) has the energy of the inversion splitting, which we set to 0.757685 \ . Therefore the \ energies, which are based on a different contention for the lowest ro-vibrational energy, are shifted by 0.757685 \ before using them in the MARVELisation procedure. For practical purposes, we keep the non-existing energies in the .states file, which are however `switched-off' from any intensity applications by setting the corresponding degeneracy factors to zero.

In Figure , an overview of the new line list is presented in a form of a room temperature ( K) stick spectrum compared to the HITRAN 2020 3 15 data . Using the MARVELised energies only, we obtain 53 856 CoYuTe-15\ transitions with intensities above cm2/molecule after scaling to terrestrial (natural) abundance as taken from hitran.org. This is to compare to 13 792 3 15 transitions in HITRAN 2020, mostly covering the wavenumber region below 3500 . Figure offers a more detailed comparison of the CoYuTe-15\ K spectrum with HITRAN 2020 in the three spectroscopic windows showing 4 bands, assuming natural abundances. This high resolution 3 15 \ line list in the HITRAN format is provided as supplementary material.

In Figure , the cumulative density of energy sources for each transition is plot as a function of transition intensity for the CoYuTe-15\ line list at 296 K. This figure demonstrates that essentially all transitions with intensity above 10-20 cm/molecule have both lower and upper energy levels accounted for in the \ analysis. Even at 10-22 cm/molecule, approximately half of the transitions are accounted for in the \ analysis.

For a detailed analysis, six strong lines from the latter work were selected, analysed and compared to the CoYuTe-15\ values in Table . The intensity ratios vary from 75\% (weaker band ) to up to \% (stronger band ). This agreement between theory and experiment shows that CoYuTe-15\ can be very useful as a starting point for formally assigning this spectrum.

 

 

Collisional line-broadening

Line-broadening parameters are an essential part of a spectroscopic database required to produce molecular cross sections. As part of the new line list for 3 15 , here we update the ammonia line line-broadening parameters applicable both to 3 14 and 3 15 . This is in line with the ExoMol line broadening `diet' adopted in . Collisional line-broadening of 3 14 has been extensively studied in the past, with a special attention to the microwave inversion spectra that exhibits its own peculiarities . A review of available broadening data in the infrared by molecular hydrogen and helium was made by HITRAN . Little vibrational dependence is observed for these perturbers, so only rotational dependence of broadening parameters is considered here. For H2 broadening coefficients , a polynomial in and , first appearing in , is adopted to describe the rotational dependence in terms of quantum number and rotational line index . We introduce one change to this polynomial, namely using the absolute value of instead of to ensure correct behaviour in P- and R-branches. The spectroscopic coefficient is defined as for P-branch and for R-branch.

The single-power law is assumed for the temperature dependence of :
 
where is the temperature exponent and is the half-width-at-half-maximum (HWHM) of the line at the reference temperature.

Here we provide two ExoMol broadening `diets' for 3 in the form of .broad files. The first one uses the m0 diet represented by the -dependence only with dependencies on all other quantum numbers averaged. We also give a version with the m1 diet, with , i.e. and dependent, which should provide a more accurate description of 3 line shapes broadened by H2. Although broadening coefficients for various can substantially differ (see Fig. ), the non-rigorous quantum rotational number is not always well characterised and therefore the m0 diet can be more practical. Wherever this assignment is known and reliable, the m1 diet can be used as a more complete representation of the broadening parameters.

Helium-broadening parameters show little variation even against the rotational variable , so the m0 diet should suffice. The m1 diet for this broadener is still provided for the sake of completeness.
To construct the m-diets helium-broadening, the HITRAN data for and were used. These data are based on 3 14 measurements, but should also work well for broadening of 3 15 . estimate that the broadening parameters for both isotopologues are within 3\% at the measured temperatures; this is well below the uncertainties associated with the current parametrisations of and .

 

Conclusions

An extensive ExoMol line list CoYuTe-15\ for the isotopologue of 3 15 CoYuTe-15 is presented. The line list was computed with the variational program \ using the spectroscopic model (empirical PES and \ DMS) of 3 developed and used for production of a hot line list of 3 14 by . The line list covers the wavenumber range up to 10000 \ and the rotational excitations up to . In order to improve the variational energies, a set of 2777 empirically derived energy levels of 3 15 was constructed using the \ procedure and used to replace the theoretical energies where possible. On top of that, we used an isotopologue extrapolation technique to generate energies for states not covered by the 3 15 \ but featured in the 3 14 \ set . This IE technique was applied to fill the gaps in the \ energies from the ground state, , and . We show that the line list is sufficiently accurate to help assign the existing experimental spectra of 3 15 as well to facilitate future spectroscopic analyses and astronomical detections. The line list as well as the spectroscopic model can be obtained at www.exomol.com.
